overview.body: Your save file works, but writing six separate write() calls for six variables already feels tedious. Now imagine needing to save twenty cities. Or fifty hazards. That approach doesn't scale. This week you discover the tool that makes all of that elegant: lists. You will replace your if/elif destination chains with parallel lists and index lookups, add two tracking lists that grow automatically as the player travels, and upgrade your save/load system to persist those lists. You will also write three new utility functions into function_library.py — the first time the library grows this module.

mentorQuote.quote: Every if/elif chain you write is a promise to maintain it forever. Lists let you make that promise once and never revisit it. That is what scaling looks like.

objectives: Create parallel lists to store destination names, distances, and notes
Replace if/elif destination selection with list index lookups
Build tracking lists with append() to record visited cities and hazards
Use the in operator to check whether a city has already been visited
Use len() to count list items in the trip report
Upgrade save_game() and load_game() to persist tracking lists
Add save_list_to_file(), load_list_from_file(), and file_exists() to function_library.py
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 7: Program 7-1 (list with initial values), Program 7-3 (append), Program 7-2 (in operator), Program 7-9 (len), Program 7-16 (write list to file), Program 7-17 (read file into list).
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 7
programRequirements: SA_DESTINATIONS, SA_DISTANCES, and SA_NOTES parallel lists replace all hardcoded destination if/elif chains
visited_cities list tracks every city in the route, starting with San Antonio
hazards_list tracks every hazard name encountered during play
save_game() writes the enhanced format including lists and the ---HAZARDS--- separator
load_game() reads the enhanced format and restores both tracking lists
function_library.py adds save_list_to_file(), load_list_from_file(), and file_exists()

namedConstants.name: SA_DESTINATIONS
namedConstants.description: List of destination city names reachable from San Antonio

namedConstants.name: SA_DISTANCES
namedConstants.description: Parallel list — miles for each destination in SA_DESTINATIONS

namedConstants.name: SA_NOTES
namedConstants.description: Parallel list — short descriptor for each destination (e.g. 'BEACH!', 'Gateway East')

overview.filenameNote: Submit lab-3-2.py and function_library.py this week.
overview.additionalFilesNote: function_library.py is submitted alongside lab-3-2.py this week — it gets three new file utility functions. CodeGrade tests those functions in isolation.
overview.saveYourCode: Keep both files — Module 3.3 builds directly on this code, adding sorted hazard display and a trip report file.

rows.task: Add SA_DESTINATIONS, SA_DISTANCES, SA_NOTES and update display_destinations()
Replace if/elif destination selection with index lookup
Add visited_cities and hazards_list with append() and in operator
Upgrade save_game() and load_game() to include tracking lists
Add 3 file functions to function_library.py and enhance trip report
Test the full cycle, verify save format, submit both files

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here is how your finished program fits together. The [NEW] tags show what you are adding this week. The [REPLACED] tags show what changes from your 3.1 code.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- CONSTANTS ----
SAVE_FILE = 'savegame.txt'           (unchanged)
SA_DESTINATIONS = [...]              [NEW — replaces destination if/elif]
SA_DISTANCES    = [...]              [NEW — parallel list for miles]
SA_NOTES        = [...]              [NEW — parallel list for city notes]
... (other constants unchanged)

# ---- HELPER FUNCTIONS ----
display_destinations(city)           [REPLACED — now uses list loop]
get_destination(city, choice)        [NEW — index lookup returns (dest, dist)]

# ---- SAVE / LOAD FUNCTIONS ----
save_game(...)                       [REPLACED — now saves lists too]
load_game()                          [REPLACED — now loads lists too]

# ---- GAME STATE ----
visited_cities  = ['San Antonio']    [NEW — tracking list]
hazards_list    = []                 [NEW — tracking list]

MAIN MENU:  (unchanged from 3.1)
│
GAME LOOP:
│
│── display_destinations()          [REPLACED — loops the list]
│── get_destination()                [NEW]
│
│   TRAVEL LOOP:
│   │── Roll D20, move miles
│   │── Hazard check → append to hazards_list  [NEW]
│   │── Repeat until arrival
│
│── append(destination) to visited_cities      [NEW]
│── Save and Quit option            (unchanged)
│
FINAL TRIP REPORT:
│── Show visited_cities with len()  [REPLACED]
│── Show hazards_list with len()    [REPLACED]

# ---- function_library.py additions ----
save_list_to_file(filename, data_list)   [NEW]
load_list_from_file(filename)            [NEW]
file_exists(filename)                    [NEW]

content.body: Right now your destination options are buried in if/elif chains. You are going to replace all of that with three parallel lists — one for city names, one for distances, and one for short notes. Once those lists exist, everything that used to take a chain of branches takes a single index lookup instead.

items: Start by copying lab-3-1.py to a new file named lab-3-2.py. All work this week goes in lab-3-2.py.
Near the top of your file, after SAVE_FILE, add three new parallel list constants for San Antonio's destinations. Keep them adjacent so the parallel relationship is visually obvious:
Update your display_destinations() function so it loops through SA_DESTINATIONS and SA_DISTANCES using range(len(SA_DESTINATIONS)) to print the numbered menu, instead of hardcoded print statements.
Verify the menu still displays all four destinations in the same numbered format as before — the player-facing output should not change.

references.description: Demonstrates creating a list with initial values and looping through it with range(len()).

content.title: What parallel lists look like
content.body: Position 0 in each list belongs together, position 1 belongs together, and so on. The index is the connection between them:
items: SA_DESTINATIONS[0] is 'Corpus Christi', SA_DISTANCES[0] is 150, SA_NOTES[0] is 'BEACH!'
SA_DESTINATIONS[1] is 'Houston',       SA_DISTANCES[1] is 200, SA_NOTES[1] is 'Gateway East'
SA_DESTINATIONS[2] is 'Austin',        SA_DISTANCES[2] is 80,  SA_NOTES[2] is 'Keep it Weird'
SA_DESTINATIONS[3] is 'Laredo',        SA_DISTANCES[3] is 160, SA_NOTES[3] is 'Dead End'
SA_DESTINATIONS, SA_DISTANCES, and SA_NOTES constants defined with four entries each
display_destinations() uses a loop over the lists — no hardcoded city names in the function body
Running the program and reaching the destination menu shows the same four options as before

checkpoints.title: Replace if/elif Destination Selection with List Indexing

content.body: This is the magic moment. The if/elif chain that selected a destination based on the player's menu choice gets replaced with a single index lookup. The player's choice (1–4) maps directly to an index (0–3) via choice - 1.

items: Find the section of your game loop where the player's menu choice determined which city and distance to use. It probably looks like a chain of if choice == 1: ... elif choice == 2: ... blocks.
Replace that entire chain with index lookups. Compute the index once: index = choice - 1, then use it to pull both values: destination = SA_DESTINATIONS[index] and distance = SA_DISTANCES[index].
If your code displays a city note or tagline, pull that from SA_NOTES[index] as well.
Test by running the game and selecting each of the four destination choices in turn. The destination name, distance, and note should all be correct.

content.quote: You just deleted fifteen lines of if/elif and replaced them with two. That is not a trick — that is how professional code actually works. One lookup, any size list.

content.title: Off-by-one: choice vs index
content.body: Player choices start at 1 (because menus do), but list indices start at 0 (because Python does). Always subtract 1: index = choice - 1. If you forget, choice 1 gives you the second city, and choice 4 crashes with an IndexError.

items: The destination if/elif chain is completely removed
Destination and distance are set via SA_DESTINATIONS[index] and SA_DISTANCES[index]
All four destination choices work correctly in a test run

checkpoints.title: Add the visited_cities and hazards_list Tracking Lists

content.body: Now you add two empty lists to the game state — one that grows every time the player arrives in a city, and one that grows every time a hazard is encountered. By the time the final trip report prints, both lists contain a complete record of the journey.

items: In the game state initialization section (after the main menu, once you know whether this is a new or loaded game), add two list variables. For a new game, initialize them as: visited_cities = ['San Antonio'] and hazards_list = []. San Antonio is pre-loaded because the player starts there.
Inside the game loop, after the player arrives at their destination, append that destination to the list: visited_cities.append(destination).

Before appending, check whether the player has already visited that city using the in operator. If they have, print a message like 'You have been to Houston before!' — then still append, since the list tracks the route, not unique cities.
Inside the hazard handling block, when a hazard is triggered, append the hazard name string to hazards_list: hazards_list.append(hazard).

In the final trip report, replace any hardcoded stat output with list-based display. Loop through visited_cities using range(len(visited_cities)) to print a numbered route. Use len(hazards_list) to report the hazard count.

references.description: Shows creating an empty list and adding items dynamically with append().
Demonstrates using the in operator to check whether an item exists in a list.
Shows len() and other built-in functions for summarizing list contents.

items: visited_cities starts as ['San Antonio'] on a new game
Each destination arrival appends to visited_cities
The in operator prints a revisit message when applicable
Each hazard trigger appends to hazards_list
Final trip report loops through both lists and uses len() for counts

checkpoints.title: Upgrade save_game() and load_game() for the Enhanced Format

content.body: Your save file currently holds six lines of scalar data. This checkpoint upgrades it to also persist the two tracking lists. Cities go after the six stats, followed by a ---HAZARDS--- separator line, followed by hazards. The load function reads cities until it hits the separator, then reads hazards until the file ends.

items: Update save_game() to accept two additional parameters: visited_cities and hazards_list. Write the six scalar variables exactly as before.
After the six scalars, loop through visited_cities and write each city on its own line.
Write the separator line: outfile.write('---HAZARDS---\n')
Loop through hazards_list and write each hazard on its own line. Then close the file.

Update load_game() to read back the two lists. After reading the six scalars, read cities into a local list using a while loop — keep reading until you hit the separator line '---HAZARDS---' or an empty string.
After the separator, read hazards into a second local list using another while loop until you reach an empty string (end of file).
Return a tuple that now includes all eight values: the six scalars plus the two lists.
Update the main menu's Load Game branch to unpack all eight values from the tuple.

references.description: Demonstrates writing a list to a file by looping and writing each item with str().
Shows the while line != '' pattern for reading variable-length data from a file.

content.title: What the enhanced save file looks like
content.body: After saving a game where Maria traveled San Antonio → Austin → Houston and hit two hazards, the file should look like this:
items: Maria
Houston
San Antonio
Austin
Houston
---HAZARDS---
Buc-ee's appears!
Speed trap!
save_game() writes all six scalars, then the cities list, then ---HAZARDS---, then the hazards list
load_game() reads all eight values back correctly and returns them as a tuple
A full save-quit-reload cycle restores the player's name, city, stats, route, and hazard history
Opening savegame.txt in a text editor shows the expected multi-section format

checkpoints.title: Add File Functions to function_library.py and Enhance the Trip Report

content.body: The final checkpoint this week has two parts. First, you add three reusable file utility functions to function_library.py — CodeGrade tests these in isolation. Second, you update the final trip report to display the route from visited_cities and the full hazard list from hazards_list.

items: Open function_library.py. Add a new section comment at the bottom: # FILE FUNCTIONS (Added in Lab 3-2)
Add save_list_to_file(filename, data_list) — opens the file in write mode using the with statement, loops through the list, and writes each item as a string with '\n' appended.

Add load_list_from_file(filename) — opens the file in read mode using the with statement, reads line by line with a while line != '' loop, strips '\n' from each line with rstrip(), appends to a result list, and returns it. Wraps the whole body in try-except FileNotFoundError and returns an empty list if the file does not exist.
Add file_exists(filename) — attempts to open the file in read mode inside a try block and returns True. Returns False in the except FileNotFoundError block.
All three functions must have complete docstrings.

In lab-3-2.py, update the final trip report section. Display the cities route as a numbered list by looping through visited_cities with range(len(visited_cities)). Display each hazard from hazards_list with a bullet. Use len() for the total counts.

references.description: Pattern for the save_list_to_file() function body — loop and write each item.
Pattern for reading a file into a list — readlines() approach shown here, but use the while readline() loop in your library for consistency with 3.1.

content.title: Use with statement in the library functions
content.body: The three new library functions must use the with statement to open files — with open(filename, 'w') as outfile:. This is the Chapter 6.3 pattern. Using open() and close() directly in library functions is acceptable in your main game file but not in the library.

items: Run the program. Play through a full game — travel to at least two cities, trigger at least one hazard.
Let the game end normally. Verify the final trip report shows a numbered city route and a bulleted hazard list.
Run again and choose Save and Quit after one turn. Reload and verify the full route and hazard history are restored.
Check that function_library.py has all three new functions with docstrings and that they import without errors.
function_library.py has save_list_to_file(), load_list_from_file(), and file_exists(), each with a docstring
All three library functions use the with statement
Final trip report displays the full city route and hazard list from the tracking lists
Both files pass a syntax check (run python3 function_library.py — no errors)

beforeYouSubmit: lab-3-2.py has a top-of-file comment block with your name, abc123, section number, and a description
function_library.py has all three new functions — save_list_to_file(), load_list_from_file(), and file_exists() — each with a docstring
SA_DESTINATIONS, SA_DISTANCES, and SA_NOTES parallel lists are defined as constants
Destination selection uses index lookup (choice - 1), not if/elif
visited_cities starts as ['San Antonio'] on a new game and grows with append()
hazards_list starts as [] and each hazard trigger appends to it
save_game() writes the enhanced format including both lists and the ---HAZARDS--- separator
load_game() reads back all eight values (six scalars plus two lists) correctly
Library functions use the with statement for file handling
finalChecklist.scopeReminder: Chapters 6–7 are both in scope this week. The with statement is permitted in function_library.py (it is introduced in Chapter 6.3). Do NOT use sort(), copy(), or any Chapter 7 techniques not yet covered — those are saved for Lab 3.3. Do NOT use dictionaries, sets, classes, or any Chapter 8+ features.

permittedTechniques.chapters: 2–7 (all techniques from Chapters 2–6 plus Chapter 7 list creation, append, in operator, index access, len, and file/list patterns)
specific: List creation with initial values: SA_DESTINATIONS = ['Corpus Christi', ...]
while line != '' loop for reading files
permittedTechniques.notPermitted: sort(), copy(), remove(), index() method, dictionaries, sets, classes, list comprehensions

warnings: Submit both lab-3-2.py and function_library.py — CodeGrade tests the library functions independently. A missing function_library.py will fail those tests even if your main file is correct.
finalChecklist.saveYourCode: Keep both files — Lab 3.3 builds on this code to add sorted hazard display, unique city counting, and a formatted trip report written to a file.

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read Program 7-1 for the parallel list pattern and Program 7-3 for append() — these two programs together cover most of what this lab introduces
If the save/load cycle breaks, open savegame.txt in a text editor and count the lines — the format must match the spec exactly, with ---HAZARDS--- as the separator
Submit to CodeGrade early — it runs the library functions in isolation so you can see which specific function is failing
Come to Zoom or office hours — list and file bugs are easy to spot together on a screen share
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 6 and Chapter 7 BookEx Reference Guides — both chapters are in scope this week
